what_it_does: Documents runtime constraints and reliability caveats for message/event delivery through Claude Code channel memory, aimed at users building always-on or confirmation-sensitive workflows.
observations: Events are delivered only while a session is open.
Delivery is acknowledged at the transport layer, not when Claude has processed the event.
If the session has not loaded the channel or a policy blocks it, events are dropped silently with no server-side error.
Queued events are batched and delivered together at the next turn boundary, without interrupting in-flight tool calls.
Channel events fire only in interactive sessions, not in headless (-p) mode.
The author states the headless limitation was proven in their own environment.
Applications must implement their own delivery confirmation using state tracking plus a reply tool.
standouts: Silent drop behavior when the channel is not loaded or policy blocks it.
Channels do not work in headless (-p) sessions.
Queue-to-turn batching rather than immediate per-event delivery.
Delivery confirmation is the consumer's responsibility.
conventions: Uses YAML frontmatter with id, address, type, source, state, title, author_session, channel, links, created/updated timestamps, and history.
Uses board:// URI for item identity and part_of links.
Body is a Markdown bullet list under a single heading.
Timestamps are ISO 8601 with timezone offset.
concerns: The headless caveat is supported only by an environment-specific claim ('proven in our own environment') rather than a reproducible test or versioned behavior.
No mitigation strategy is provided for silent drops beyond generic state tracking.
The phrase 'policy blocks it' is used without defining the policy mechanism.
notes: A reliability notice that channel events are best-effort, silently dropped under several conditions, and require application-level confirmation.
questions: What specific transport underlies the 'written to transport' acknowledgment?
What is the exact 'reply tool' recommended for delivery confirmation?
Which policy system can block channel loading?
How is a 'stream' defined and why must separate streams use separate sessions?
Is the headless limitation tied to a specific Claude Code version or platform?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches: the title frames the item as delivery constraints for reliability, and the body enumerates exactly those constraints.
apparent_themes: Operational caveats for interactive vs headless usage
intention_signals: It appears to be trying to set realistic expectations about channel reliability and steer consumers toward building explicit confirmation logic rather than assuming at-least-once or exactly-once delivery.
novelty: Explicitly documents silent event drops and the headless-mode channel limitation, which are non-obvious runtime behaviors.
contribution: Provides a concise, file-local record of delivery semantics and consumer responsibilities that a fused system would keep as an operational warning.
summary_for_embedding: A noticeboard item listing reliability constraints for Claude Code channel message delivery, including silent drops, session-open requirements, turn-boundary batching, and the headless-mode limitation.
intention_for_embedding: To warn developers that channel event delivery is best-effort and requires custom confirmation logic for reliable workflows.
